aliases: Oat and fruit bar with yoghurt coating

english: Oat and fruit bar with yoghurt coating

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for yoghurt-coated muesli bar; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: yoghurt coating (sugar, vegetable fat, milk solids, yoghurt powder)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard yoghurt-coated muesli bar weighs approximately 35 g and is typically consumed as a single snack or breakfast accompaniment.

nutrient_source.title: Muesli bar, yoghurt-coated — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F05050016 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry 'Granola bar, soft, coated with nonchocolate coating' (NDB 19016) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; the estimate of 7.5 g/100g is consistent with palm-based yoghurt coating composition.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for yoghurt-coated muesli bar found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 61 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: 'Muesli bar, chewy, with choc chips or fruit' (GI ~61, medium) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Yoghurt coating adds sugar and fat but does not substantially alter GI relative to the oat-and-fruit base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (61) × available carbohydrate per serving (35 g × 59.5 g/100g = 20.825 g) / 100 = 12.7, rounded to 12.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (23.1 g/100g) with insulin-mediated sodium retention risk; sodium itself is moderate at 74 mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: Although sodium is low at 74 mg/100g, the very high sugar content (23.1 g/100g) promotes insulin-driven sodium retention and sympathetic nervous system activation, which can independently elevate blood pressure; hypertensive patients should limit or avoid this product per AHA 2018 dietary guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 23.1 g/100g exceeds the 20 g/100g T2DM hard gate; high total carbohydrate (59.5 g/100g) and medium-high GI (61) drive significant postprandial glucose excursions
type2_diabetes.guidance: With sugar at 23.1 g/100g and total carbohydrate at 59.5 g/100g, this product is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; the yoghurt coating provides no meaningful probiotic benefit and contributes primarily sugar and saturated fat, and the product should be avoided in favour of lower-GI, lower-sugar snack alternatives per ADA 2023 Standards of Care.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~7.5 g/100g from palm-based yoghurt coating exceeds the 5 g/100g CVD threshold; high sugar also contributes to dyslipidaemia risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The palm-based yoghurt coating contributes an estimated 7.5 g/100g saturated fat, which exceeds the AHA recommended threshold for cardiovascular patients; combined with 23.1 g/100g sugar that promotes triglyceride elevation, individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this product and choose lower-saturated-fat snack alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar 23.1 g/100g exceeds the 15 g/100g RA threshold; estimated saturated fat ~7.5 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold; both promote pro-inflammatory cytokines
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The combination of high sugar (23.1 g/100g) and high saturated fat (~7.5 g/100g from palm-based yoghurt coating) promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine activity; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this product per EULAR dietary guidance.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A yoghurt-coated muesli bar is a processed snack bar made from rolled oats, dried fruits (such as sultanas, apricots, or cranberries), sugars, glucose syrup, and vegetable oils, encased in a yoghurt-flavoured compound coating. The coating is typically made from sugar, palm-based vegetable fat, milk solids, and yoghurt powder — it is not equivalent to plain yoghurt and provides no live probiotic cultures. The bar has a chewy, dense texture from the compressed oat and fruit base, with a sweet, slightly tangy outer shell from the yoghurt coating. It is commonly consumed as a breakfast accompaniment or mid-morning snack in Australia, the United Kingdom, Singapore, and other markets where Western-style convenience snacks are popular. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 398 kcal, 6.6 g protein, 13.9 g fat (estimated 7.5 g saturated from palm-based coating), 59.5 g carbohydrate, 23.1 g sugar, 6.4 g dietary fibre, and 74 mg sodium. The high sugar content (23.1 g/100g) makes this product unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. The estimated saturated fat from the palm-based coating raises cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis concerns. Despite the wholegrain oat base and reasonable fibre content, the ultra-processed nature and high sugar load limit its clinical suitability for most chronic disease populations. The glycemic index is estimated at 61 (medium), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 12.8 per standard 35 g serving.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F05050016, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous entry and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous muesli bar entry and documented. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source for this item): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this food item and are not available in a directly matching approved secondary source. Saturated fat (7.5 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 19016 'Granola bar, soft, coated with nonchocolate coating' as the closest approved analogous entry; this value is consistent with the palm-based vegetable fat composition typical of commercial yoghurt coatings. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest muesli bar entry; the yoghurt coating does not substantially alter the GI of the oat-and-fruit base. No zero-value macros are present; all core macros are non-zero and sourced. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; no mandatory non-English local-language names apply under G6 multilingual rules. The food_group has been reclassified from 'Grains and staples / Grain-based dishes' to 'Snacks / Cereal and grain-based snack bars' to better reflect the product's commercial snack nature.
